coredata.dc:title: Transformation of oil palm biomass to optical carbon quantum dots by carbonisation-activation and low temperature hydrothermal processes
coredata.dc:creator: Mahat, Nur Akma
Shamsudin, Siti Aisyah
coredata.dc:description: Large amounts of biomass produced by the palm oil industry are either burnt in open air or disposed in waste ponds and contribute to global climate. Therefore, these biomasses should be utilised to obtain functional materials, such as carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are facile, cheap and environmentally friendly. This study has successfully converted the biomass from oil palm empty fruit bunch-derived activated carbon to CQDs through low-cost processes, involving carbonisation–activation and low-temperature hydrothermal carbonisation. The obtained CQDs have spherical shapes with diameter range of 5–7 nm and exhibit excellent water stability and solubility properties due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. CQDs are amorphous carbon structures due to high-temperature treatment during carbonisation–activation and exhibit the Raman D and G bands, conforming to the formation of low-dimensional carbon nanostructure. The optical properties of CQDs show that Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) are in strong blue absorption and exhibit high excitation–emission dependence for photoluminescence. MTT assay has revealed that these CQDs have low toxicity and favourable biocompatibility. Therefore, CQDs prepared from oil palm biomass possess excellent optical properties, water stability and solubility and may have a wide range of applications, such as in bioimaging, antioxidation and pH sensors.

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbonisation-activation process of oil palm biomass

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work has been supported by the Research University Grant ( GUP-2017-109 ) from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia and Fundamental Research Grant ( FRGS/1/2014/SG06/UKM/03/1 ) from Ministry of Education Malaysia .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM image of OPEFBs biomass, (b) SEM image of AC-OPEFBs, (c) TEM image (inset) distribution of histogram and (d) HRTEM image of synthesis CQDs with lattice spacing of d = 0.32 nm.
(a) Size distribution of CQDs by DLS intensity (7 ± 1 nm) and (b) XRD pattern of CQDs at 2θ = 18.5° and 2θ = 21.92°.
(a) FTIR spectrum of AC-OPEFBs and CQDs (b) Raman spectrum shows D band and G band of CQDs with an intensity ratio ID/IG of approximately 0.84. (c) High resolution XPS spectra of C1s and (d) O1s.

(a) UV–vis spectrum of CQDs with optical images illuminated under daylight lamp (left) and UV lamp 365 nm (right) in the inset (b) Tauc plot of CQDs with two band gap energies at n - π* (3.8 eV) and π - π* (4.5 eV). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Photoluminescence (PL) excitation and emission spectra of CQDs at 360 and 440 nm in wavelengths. (b) PL decay spectra of CQDs at 440 nm (17.21ns). (c) Optical properties mechanisms of CQDs with energy difference between HOMO and LUMO.
(a) PL intesities change of CQDs at various pH values and (b) PL intensity with pH variation when excited at 360 nm with pKa value.
MTT assay to determined CC50 for CQDs derived from oil palm biomass.
Mass changing on OPEFBs before and after carbonisation process with percentage of mass reduction.
OD value for cell viability at 96 well plate using by MTT.

head.ce:title: Transformation of oil palm biomass to optical carbon quantum dots by carbonisation-activation and low temperature hydrothermal processes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Physics, Faculty of Science and Technology, University Kebangsaan Malaysia, 43600 Bangi, Selangor, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Large amounts of biomass produced by the palm oil industry are either burnt in open air or disposed in waste ponds and contribute to global climate. Therefore, these biomasses should be utilised to obtain functional materials, such as carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which are facile, cheap and environmentally friendly. This study has successfully converted the biomass from oil palm empty fruit bunch-derived activated carbon to CQDs through low-cost processes, involving carbonisation–activation and low-temperature hydrothermal carbonisation. The obtained CQDs have spherical shapes with diameter range of 5–7 nm and exhibit excellent water stability and solubility properties due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. CQDs are amorphous carbon structures due to high-temperature treatment during carbonisation–activation and exhibit the Raman D and G bands, conforming to the formation of low-dimensional carbon nanostructure. The optical properties of CQDs show that Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) are in strong blue absorption and exhibit high excitation–emission dependence for photoluminescence. MTT assay has revealed that these CQDs have low toxicity and favourable biocompatibility. Therefore, CQDs prepared from oil palm biomass possess excellent optical properties, water stability and solubility and may have a wide range of applications, such as in bioimaging, antioxidation and pH sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Most of the biowaste residues from palm oil mill plantations are either burnt in open air or disposed in waste ponds. This phenomenon contributes to the global climate change by emitting carbon dioxide and methane. The oil palm biomass can be categorised into solid and liquid wastes, with a total of 83 and 60 million tonnes of waste generated in 2012, respectively [1]. This phenomenon will increase the solid and liquid wastes to 85–110 and 70–110 million tonnes, respectively, by year 2020 [1]. However, most of the biomass is left in plantations, demonstrating the underutilisation of the important lignocellulosic feedstock [1]. The biomass generated at the mill from the processing of 95.38 million tonnes of fresh fruit bunches harvested in 2014, in accordance with the percentage composition, will lead to the generation of 7.34 million tonnes of empty fruit bunches (EFBs) [1]. The increasing awareness on pollution has led to the development of solutions for environmental problems by maximising the utilisation of the abundant biomass for the synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Biomass, especially from agricultural byproducts, is a rich source of cellulosic material. Biomass contains an average composition of 40%–50% cellulose, 20%–30% hemicellulose, 20%–25% lignin and 1%–5% ash and exhibits excellent water solubility, making them attractive sources of CQDs [2]. Various raw materials from biomass, including grass [3], rice husks [4], coconut shells and husk [5], coffee seeds [6], banana leaves or peels [7], lemon and watermelon peels [8,9], have been used as carbon sources. In addition, the fruit extract can be used as carbon source and can be applied in bioimaging, as fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions and fluorescent ink [10,11]. However, the biomass from oil palm EFBs (OPEFBs) has attracted our attention due to the eco-friendliness of lignocellulosic materials [2] and its main advantages, including availability and low cost, making this biomass a suitable precursor for CQDs preparation.

CQDs, a new class of carbon-based nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm [12], are extensively utilised due to their excellent photostability, high aqueous solubility, robust chemical inertness and ease of modification. CQDs also exhibit good biological properties, such as favourable biocompatibility, good water solubility and low toxicity [13]. These properties provide the wide range of applications of CQDs in water purification, biomedicine and device storage [14]. CQDs can also be prepared from polymers, [15,16] petroleum residues, [17,18] and biomass materials [19,20]. Biomass materials, such as the byproducts from agricultural and residual wastes, are the most attractive carbon sources for the synthesis of CQDs due to the excellent properties of carbon-based materials from biomass, such as low toxicity, large surface area, high conductivity and long life. Although biomass waste has high carbon contents, only a small percentage (20–30%) are consumed for human daily activities and agriculture, i.e., recycling, composting or combustion, whereas the rest (70–80%) are disposed into landfills and cause environmental hazards to the ecosystem, living things and plants [21]. Thus, the carbon-based biomass can be considered as a potential precursor to produce CQDs with new functionalities and grow sustainable materials in various applications.

Various approaches, including arc-discharge, electrochemical oxidation, laser ablation, pyrolysis, acid dehydration, ultrasonication, microwave pyrolysis and carbonisation, have been reported for the synthesis of carbonaceous materials into CQDs [12]. However, most of these methods provide adverse circumstances, such as the need for a large amount of strong acids for self-passivation, expensive carbon sources, complex reaction, time-consuming process and involvement of sophisticated instruments and tedious procedures. Furthermore, intensive research and techniques for the synthesis of carbon-based materials, such as pyrolysis carbonisation and hydrothermal method, have been implemented to convert biomass into functionalised products [21]. Carbonisation (pyrolysis) and hydrothermal method are employed to produce carbon-based materials from biomass. Recently, most researchers have used the hydrothermal method to synthesise CQDs by using several biomasses or waste as reported by Shen et al. [22], who adopted the hydrothermal carbonisation technique to synthesise CQDs by using low-cost wastes as precursors. Chunduri et al. [5] used the single-step synthesis of CQDs from coconut shell biomass via hydrothermal carbonisation. Wang et al. [23] synthesised CQDs from rice husk biomass via hydrothermal method to achieve the comprehensive utilisation of rice husks. Joseph et al. [24] also synthesised CQDs from coffee husk for marking water pathway in plants. Atchudan et al. and Arul et al. [25,26] used a one-step hydrothermal method to synthesise highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots by using fruit extract for cell imaging and catalytic applications. The synthesised CQDs from various biomass carbon precursors produce CQDs with different characteristics [14]. Hence, more work is required for the commercialisation of biomass-derived carbon materials for sufficient performance, activity and effectiveness.

Carbonisation involves a slow pyrolysis process, in which biomass is converted into a highly carbonaceous material [21]. Typically, carbonisation involves heating the biomass in an oxygen-free or oxygen-limited environment. Furthermore, the hydrothermal method is another promising technique for the conversion of biomass into novel carbon materials and involves the thermochemical degradation of biomass in the presence of water at elevated temperature and pressure [21]. On the basis of the reaction mechanism and experimental conditions, the hydrothermal method can be classified into two major categories, namely, the high- (400 °C–800 °C) and the low-temperature (200 °C–300 °C) hydrothermal processes. The low-temperature hydrothermal process is environmentally friendly, can synthesise numerous carbon materials with various morphology, size, shape and surface functionalities and employs several chemical transformations, making it suitable for the synthesis of CQDs.

CQDs are an emerging fluorescent subclass of the carbon nanomaterial family that have been known as a versatile new platform in various applications, such as in sensing, bioimaging, photodynamic therapy and catalysis. To improve the reliability of detection, Dou and co-workers have developed a dual-ratiometric response fluorescent probe, which is equipped with MPIBA and shows large stoke, absorbance and emission shifts and ultrafast and high sensitivity, making it suitable for biological monitoring [27–29]. Moreover, as an alternative technology, the doping of CQDs with other elements is a highly efficient way to tune the fluorescence properties of CQDs by adjusting the electronic characteristic, surface and chemical structures. Sun and Lei used microwave-assisted synthesis of nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots with dual fluorescence emission, enhancing the fluorescence effect [30].

Nonetheless, biocompatibility and bioviability are crucial factors that must be considered during the preparation of CQDs for biological application, such as in live cells, tissues and animals. Therefore, the biocompatibility of CQDs was evaluated in this study by using an in vitro cytotoxicity assay. The Vero cells used in this study were isolated from kidney epithelial cells extracted from an African green monkey, which were split into several different species. The Vero cells are widely used in assessing the cytotoxicity of the verotoxin-producing Escherichia coli strains. In addition, the cytotoxicity effects play an important role in the determination of the applicability of CQDs in biological properties. Wang [23] reported the cytotoxicity from rice husk graphene quantum dots on HeLa and MCF-7 cells, resulting in cell viability of >90% (HeLa cells) and > 94% (MCF-7 cells) at a concentration of <0.5 mg/ml for QDs. Wang [12] also reported that CQDs are apparently nontoxic to cells even at a relatively high concentration of 0.4 mg/ml. Thus, the study of cytotoxicity properties is important for the biological applications of CQDs.

This study aims to utilise the lignocellulosic biomass from OPEFBs for the production of activated carbon (AC) precursor to synthesise CQDs without any doping or coating with ligand as an alternative to solve waste issues on biomass, especially in the oil palm industries. Furthermore, the morphological, structural, optical and biocompatibility properties of the synthesised CQDs from OPEFB-derived AC have been investigated. Moreover, this study focuses on the production of CQDs by converting biomass into AC to obtain CQDs via carbonisation–activation and hydrothermal carbonisation processes with low temperature, cost and energy consumption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbonisation-activation process of oil palm biomass
ce:section.ce:para: The oil palm empty fruit bunches (OPEFBs) were dried at 105 °C for 24 h. The oil palm biomass was carbonised and activated in a furnace. The dried OPEFBs were loaded onto a crucible, heated to 700 °C at a rate of 10 °C/min and held for 2 h under N2 flow. The sample was allowed to cool to room temperature in an inert atmosphere. Then, char (10 g) was placed into the furnace under N2 flow for 15 min prior to activation. The activation temperature was to 800 °C under N2 flow until the N2 was switched to CO2. The activation temperature and CO2 flow rate were kept constant for 30 min until the AC cooled down to room temperature under N2 flow.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesised from oil palm-based activated carbon through hydrothermal carbonisation with low temperature process. In brief, 6 g of oil palm-activated carbon powder was added to 150 mL of deionised water. The mixture was then transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and sealed tightly to maintain the inbuilt pressure. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 3 h. The reaction vessel was allowed to cool to room temperature, and the solution was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 30 min. The solution was filtered through 0.2 μm filter to remove micron-sized particles. The final solution was lyophilised to obtain solid CQDs. The CQDs were dispersed in DI water at a concentration of 7.6 mg/mL and stored at 4 °C for further characterisation.

The morphology of the OPEFBs and AC were analysed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) model PHILIPS XL 30 and the microstructure of CQDs were conducted by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) model JEM-2100F using an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The HRTEM and TEM samples were prepared by drop-casting CQD solutions on a carbon-coated copper grid and drying them under room temperature before analysis. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) was performed using a Zeta-Meter System 4.0. Raman spectral measurements were performed using Thermo Scientific I Model: DXR2xi Raman spectrometer with 532 nm laser. The Raman sample was prepared by dropping the solution of CQDs on a glass slide and then drying it before analysis. Raman spectroscopy was employed to observe the crystalline nature and degree of sp2 and sp3 hybridisation in carbon materials. The amorphous nature and interlayer spacing of the carbon dots were measured by an X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) (Bruker/D8 Advance). XRD is a rapid analytical technique primarily used for the phase identification of amorphous or crystalline material and can provide information on unit cell dimensions. The absorption spectra and photometric studies were performed on a Shimadzu UV-1700 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The steady state photoluminescence (PL) and decay were obtained using fluorescence spectrometer on a Perkin Elmer spectrophotometer equipped with lamp in the range of 320–400 nm as excitation wavelength sources. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to analyse the activated carbon (AC) and synthesis CQDs for their functional groups. FTIR spectrum (Model GX) used transmission mode ion range 400–4000 cm− 1 with accumulation of 32 scans and 2.0 cm resolution, whereas the elemental and compositional analysis of CQDs was measured by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using a Kratos/Axis Ultra DLD.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of CQDs was measured via 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5 diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. The test for cytotoxicity of CQDs was performed using Vero cells and 96-well plates. First, sample treatment was performed in plate design of 4 replicated per concentration containing cells and replace with 100 μL of growth medium and used a positive control. Four wells containing Vero cells were replaced with 100 μL diethylphosphate (DMSO) and used as negative controls. After treatment of cells with CQDs for 48 h, the treatment was removed from each well and replaced with MTT solutions. The stock MTT solution (5 mg/mL) was diluted to a complete growth medium with a ratio of 1:2. The diluted MTT solution (150 μL) was added to each test well and incubated for 3 h. Subsequently, 100 μL DMSO was added to each test well and shaken for 5 min. Optical density (OD) readings were obtained at 540 nm, and the percentage of cell viability was calculated.

The oil palm biomass was prepared via carbonisation–activation to produce AC as a precursor for the synthesis of CQDs through low-temperature hydrothermal carbonisation by reducing the size of AC into nanoscale. During the carbonisation of OPEFB biomass, the carbon materials were oxygenated. This process is crucial to yield the precursor for the synthesis of CQDs [31]. The morphology of OPEFBs obtained by scanning electron microscopy is shown in Fig. 1a. OPEFBs have a rigid surface with a layer of composite materials with a matrix (lignin) and reinforcement or fibre (cellulose and hemicellulose), in which the matrix material covers the whole surface of the fibre. The AC produced after carbonisation–activation process is shown in Fig. 1b. The surfaces of AC are rough and have dense ridges due to the activation of carbon oxide during the production of AC [32]. Subsequently, the structure of synthesised CQDs from AC–OPEFBs by hydrothermal treatment was confirmed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1c. The image shows a uniform dispersion of CQDs with spherical shapes with an average diameter of 5–7 nm, as shown in the histogram (inset). The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 1d) shows that the lattice space of CQDs is 0.32 nm. CQDs have a wide size range but is still below 10 nm because they do not undergo Ostwald ripening [33].

In contrast, dynamic light scattering (DLS) shows that the average size range of CQDs is within 5–10 nm with a corresponding peak of 7 ± 1 nm as shown in Fig. 2a. This contradiction occurs because DLS measures the hydrodynamic size of the particles, which is larger than the actual size of the CQDs as determined by TEM [25]. The X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) pattern shows the CQDs in an amorphous structure as indicated by the peaks (100) and (001) at bands 2θ = 18.5° and 2θ = 21.92°, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2b. Either amorphous or nanocrystalline structures of CQDs are comprised from their core with sp2 graphitic [34,35]. High-temperature treatment during carbonisation enhances the formation of amorphous, porous graphitic carbon structures [36,37]. The main diffraction peak in the XRD spectrum appears at 21.92° with a full width at half maximum of approximately 5.1°, which corresponds to an interlayer distance of 0.35 nm between the planar carbon-based sheets for graphite structure region [38,39]. In addition, Hidayu [40] reported that the activated carbon from OPEFBs displays two broad diffraction peaks at 2θ = 20-30o and 40-50o revealed the presence of amorphous structure which is disorderly stacked up by carbon rings and helpful for producing well-defined adsorbent.

Table 1 presents the change in mass of OPEFBs, indicating their mass reduction after carbonisation. The mass of OPEFBs has decreased by 43%, and the appropriate percentage for mass reduction to produce a high density of AC should be in the range of 25%–45% [2]. OPEFBs are lignocellulosic materials containing 45%–50% of cellulose, 25%–35% of hemicellulose and 25%–35% of lignin [2]. According to Abbas et al. [21], the pyrolysis or carbonisation process for lignocellulosic materials occur in stages. Hemicellulose is decomposed at 200 °C–260 °C, followed by cellulose and lignin at 240 °C–350 °C and 280 °C–500 °C, respectively. Hence, in this research, the primary pyrolysis involves burning up to 400 °C–800 °C to confirm that all lignocellulosic materials are transformed into char. Afterwards, the char is treated in a second process called activation to produce carbon, leading to the generation of pores in the micrometre scale [2]. During activation, high carbon content is generated due to the use of high temperature, causing volatile contents to be released. Hence, the possibility to increase the amount of AC is high.

Infrared (IR) spectroscopy was performed to investigate the chemical structure and functional groups of the AC-OPEFBs and CQDs. Fig. 3a shows the IR transitions of CQDs at 896, 1025, 1245 and 1595 cm− 1 are assigned to the (CC), (CO), (C=C) and (C=O), respectively [41,42]. The strong and broad absorption peak at 3309 cm− 1 corresponding to the stretching vibrational characteristic of (OH) for CQDs. This is due to the higher aqueous component after hydrothermal synthesis process of CQDs. However, most of the functional group of AC-OPEFBs are still remained even though there has transformed the size into nanoscale by hydrothermal process does not destruct the properties of AC. CQDs exhibit excellent water stability and solubility owing to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. Thus, CQDs act as a functionalised hydrophilic surface and show potential biological and many other applications.

The Raman spectroscopy shown in Fig. 3b displays two peaks of amorphous carbon of CQDs showing D and G bands at 1350 (sp3-hybridized) and 1520 cm− 1 (sp2-hybridized), respectively, with an intensity ratio ID/IG of approximately 0.84 that suggests the presence of a disordered graphite structure in CQDs [43]. According to Qu et al. [44] these peaks show the characteristic of CQDs where the G band arises from the vibrations of sp2 bonded carbon atoms corresponding to the E2g mode in the 2D hexagonal lattice of graphite. The E2g mode is a doubly degenerate Raman active optical vibration, where the carbon atoms move in graphite plane [45]. On the basis of G band intensity, CQDs are predominantly of graphitic nature. The D band is associated with the vibrations of carbon atom with incomplete bonds in the termination plane, corresponding to the disordered structure in the sp3 cluster of graphite; therefore, CQDs likely fall into the category of multi-crystalline or amorphous carbon [5].

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to determine the functionals groups and elemental compositions on the surfaces of CQDs. The XPS spectra for C 1s of CQDs shown in Fig. 3c reveal three types of carbon bonds such as CC, CO and CO at 284.5, 286.1 and 288.2 eV, respectively, which correspond to sp3 and sp2 carbons. The presence of CO and CO reveals the hydrophilic surface functionalization of CQDs, which is consistent with FTIR analysis. Fig. 3d showed the O 1s spectrum exhibit three peaks at 531.91, 532.97 and 533.01 eV which is at C-OH, C–O-C and CO respectively. However, the spectra for O 1s on CQDs possess high amounts of oxygen mainly due to the surface oxidation of organic compounds on the surface of CQDs [42,46].

The ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum of CQDs is blue-shifted as shown in Fig. 4a. The first absorbance band located at 206 nm is attributed to π-π* transition of the conjugated CC and CC bonds of carbon core of sp2, and the second absorbance band at 282 nm is due to n-π* transition of CO moieties of the surface groups [47,48]. Blue CQDs prepared through various methods show two absorption peaks in the range of 228–282 nm [51]. However, CQDs typically show strong optical absorption in the UV region (230–320 nm) with a tail extending into the visible range. For carbon core, a maximum peak of carbon at 230 nm is ascribed to π-π* transition of aromatic CC and CC bonds, whereas a shoulder at 300 nm is attributed to n-π* transition of CO bonds or other connected groups [49,50].

The optical band gap was calculated using absorption edge values (λ edge in nm) from the absorption spectrum and using equation band gap energy Eg = hc/λ, where h = plank constant = 6.626 × 10− 34 Js− 1, c = speed of light = 3.0 × 108 ms− 1, λ = cut off wavelength/absorption edge in nm and 1 eV = 1.6 × 10− 19 J. Tauc's relation was used to calculate the optical energy band gap (Eg) αhv = k(hv – E
g ) n , where α is absorption coefficient, hv is the energy of incident light, Eg is optical band gap and n = 2 and n = 1/2 are for indirect and direct band gaps, respectively [41,45]. He [51] mentions that some allotropes of carbon, such as S carbon, Z-ACA, ZCACB, C carbon, fullerite C24, T carbon, TY carbon, sp2-diamond and Amm2 carbon, are direct band gap semiconductors. All these direct band gap carbon allotropes exhibit sp3-hybridized bond and correspond with the as-prepared CQDs as revealed by FTIR and Raman analysis. Fig. 4b shows the Eg by plotting (αhv)1/2 versus hv and extrapolating the linear part of the curve by straight line to (αhv)1/2 = 0. Two calculated band gap energies are visible from the graph, the first is obtain at n - π* (3.8 eV) and the second energy at π - π* (4.5 eV).

The energy band gap for bulk materials requires minimum energy to excite the electron from the ground state to the unoccupied conduction band. In nanoscale materials, the electrons are excited to the conduction band, thus producing excitons that consist of an electron-hole pair because the absorbance energy is larger than the band gap. Afterward, these excitons are exterminated because of the emission of photons, also known as radiative recombination [34]. As defined by the Bohr radius, excitons exhibit a finite nanometre range in size [50]. If the charge carriers are smaller than the Bohr excitons' radius, then they become spatially confined within the particles. Thus, a phenomenon known as the quantum confinement, which is affected by PL spectra, occurs. Furthermore, experiencing the quantum confinement effect becomes one of the factors that determine the tuneable PL properties of CQDs due to their nanoparticle size [34].

Fig. 5a shows the excitation and emission peaks of the PL of CQDs at 360 and 440 nm wavelengths. The percentage of quantum yield (QY%) of these CQDs is given as 24.6%. The emission wavelength is in the range of 400–500 nm and appears as a blue peak at 450 nm. Emission in this range is generally caused by the electron-hole recombination or quantum confinement effect, and a similar phenomenon occurs with semiconductor quantum dots [52]. This event has affected the PL mechanism, which is involved in the quantum confinement effect on the size of particles in the intrinsic state (i.e. electron-hole recombination and quantum size effect/zigzag sites) and the surface defects on CQDs for the surface state (surface energy traps) [52,53]. The blue emission of the short wavelength is from the intrinsic state emission, whereas the green emission for the long-wavelength is from the surface states. However, these blue emission CQDs are obtained from the presence of oxygen on their surface and exist in emissive traps between n and π* [42]. Roy et al. [54] stated that the wavelength range from 280 nm to 380 nm of CQDs with high oxidation degrees shows that the excitation wavelength depends on PL properties. The CQDs with low oxidation wavelength did not show PL properties. Therefore, the excitation wavelength of CQDs prepared through carbonisation and hydrothermal treatment with high oxidation is dependent on their PL properties. Oxidation facilitates the generation of oxide defects on the surfaces of CQDs, thus introducing a complex surface state to trap excitons under excitation. Therefore, the surface states caused by radiative recombination excitation result in PL emission with corresponding energy [42,54].

The optical properties reflected by UV–Vis absorption peaks and spectra of PL intensities in Fig. 5c show the electron excitation at 4.5 eV from HOMO to LUMO, which is from π to π* at a band edge of 206 nm. The transition from n to π* displays wavelengths from 282 nm and band gap energy ~3.8 eV. This finding reveals that the PL of CQDs is highly dependent on surface defects from oxidation and emissive traps of surface functional groups (CO and C–O/C–OH) and on the intrinsic state of sp2 clusters in the CC core. This notion is supported by the observation that the PL of CQDs can be effectively quenched by both electron donors and acceptors. Therefore, the overall optical properties of CQDs are determined by the combination of the emissive sites between n and π*, and the surface state due to oxidation and intrinsic state in the carbon core by quantum confinement effects contributes to the origin of fluorescence [55]. This finding may be further explored by controlling the core sizes while maintaining the surface states.

A photo-luminescent decay curve was established to analyse the lifetime of CQDs, as shown in Fig. 5b. The fluorescence lifetime of CQDs is 17.21 ns when the lifetime decay is at 440 nm. The fluorescence lifetime increases from low to high wavelength. The excitation wavelength is dependent on the increase in fluorescence lifetime as measured from each emission decay. This phenomenon may be explained by the presence of different decay pathways and their corresponding rates of electron-hole recombination from the core and the surface states generated from photo excitation [53]. The results show that the dynamics of slow photo-luminescent decay at short-wavelength excitations (>14 ns) is due to the relaxation of the photo-generated carriers from the carbogenic core, the oxidation on the surface state, and the emissive traps of surface functional groups (CO, CO and C–OH). The rapid decay at long-wavelength excitations (~1.3 ns) is attributed to the recombination of an electron-hole pair on the surface site in the photo-generated carrier, which is directly excited from the surface state [54].

The photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. 6a) of CQDs at different pH values obtained at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The graph shows that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs is sensitive to pH. When the pH value is increased from 2 to 12 (Fig. 6b), the fluorescence intensity decreases linearly, and the fluorescence peak does not change from 440 nm as the pH value changes. The changing pH value from acidic to alkaline may be due to the protonation and deprotonation of the carboxyl groups, resulting in electrostatic charge of the CQDs and finally resulting in the Fermi level shift [5]. The excited-state pKa values of CQDs are determined in accordance with the change in fluorescence intensity at 440 nm (excited at 360 nm) and the change in pH from 2 to 12. The calculated pKa value is 7.01 on the basis of the equation pKa = pHx + log [(I
B – I
x ) / (I
x – I
A )], where I
B , I
x and I
A represent the fluorescence intensities at the absolute base form, at the chosen pH and at the absolute acid form, respectively [56].

Hydrothermal treatment was selected to synthesise CQDs from oil palm biomass without any synthetic chemical. The use of palm oil biomass for the synthesis of CQDs is suitable in representing their quantum dot size. The as-prepared CQDs exhibit a disordered graphitic structure and predominant graphitic nature that fall into the category of amorphous carbon. The water solubility properties of CQDs are due to their carboxyl and hydroxyl groups (hydrophilic). The blue emission obtained in the UV–vis spectra is due to the presence of two absorbance bands in the carbon core and surface groups of CQDs. PL analysis shows that the excitation wavelength is dependent on PL properties, such as surface defects from oxidation, emissive traps of surface functional groups and the carbon core. Moreover, the pH sensitivity exhibited by CQDs over a wide pH range (2−12) can be developed for the application of fluorescence-based pH sensor. In this study, utilising AC–OPEFBs as precursor has successfully produced high-quality CQDs with excellent characteristics and optical properties, facilitating their broad application in bioimaging, antioxidation and pH sensors.
Nur Akma Mahat: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Siti Aisyah Shamsudin: Conceptualization, Supervision, Visualization, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work has been supported by the Research University Grant (GUP-2017-109) from Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia and Fundamental Research Grant (FRGS/1/2014/SG06/UKM/03/1) from Ministry of Education Malaysia.
